skill.name: market-sizing-analysis
skill.description: Guides market sizing analysis using TAM, SAM, and SOM frameworks for startups, SaaS products, marketplaces, and business opportunities.
skill.summary: Build defensible TAM, SAM, and SOM estimates with structured market sizing methods.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but context review shows the critical SAM and weak-crypto signals are market-sizing terminology and percentage formulas. The backtick detections are Markdown code fences or inline file references, not shell execution. Residual risk is low because the skill lists external research URLs, but I found no evidence of credential access, command execution, prompt injection, or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Benign External Research Links
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded URLs to public market research, government, academic, and data provider websites. They support citation guidance and do not send data, secrets, or user input to remote services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible reference links in a data-sources guide. No reviewed file contains code or instructions that transmit credentials or user data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Market SAM Terminology
low_findings.description: The sensitive Windows SAM findings are false positives. The files use SAM as Serviceable Available Market in the TAM, SAM, and SOM market-sizing framework.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text defines SAM as Serviceable Available Market and uses it in financial formulas. I found no evidence of Windows registry hives, credential files, or password database access.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Markdown Formula Blocks
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives. The flagged backticks delimit Markdown formulas, examples, or inline documentation paths, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged ranges are Markdown fenced blocks containing market-sizing calculations or citation templates. No reviewed file contains executable scripts, shell commands, Ruby code, or dynamic command arguments.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Weak Cryptography Terms
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by market-sizing acronyms and symbols in business formulas. The reviewed files contain no hashing, encryption, signing, or cryptographic library use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss market methods, filters, percentages, and next steps. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic operations in context.

content.user_title: Size Markets with TAM, SAM, and SOM
content.value_statement: Market sizing is hard when sources, assumptions, and investor expectations are scattered. This skill guides TAM, SAM, and SOM analysis with practical methods and example structures.
actual_capabilities: Explains TAM, SAM, and SOM definitions with practical business examples.
Guides top-down, bottom-up, and value theory market sizing methods.
Shows how to apply geographic, segment, product, and adoption filters.
Provides validation steps for triangulating results across multiple sources.
Includes a worked SaaS market sizing example for investor-style analysis.
Lists research sources and citation practices for defensible assumptions.
limitations: It does not fetch live market data or verify current source availability by itself.
It depends on user-provided assumptions, source quality, and market definitions.
It gives analytical guidance, not investment, legal, or financial advice.
Several referenced example and reference files are mentioned but are not present in the scanned file tree.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Fundraising Market Slide
use_cases.description: Build a clear TAM, SAM, and SOM story with documented assumptions and validation checks.

use_cases.title: Compare Market Entry Options
use_cases.description: Estimate opportunity size across segments, regions, or customer types before choosing a launch focus.

use_cases.title: Audit Market Size Claims
use_cases.description: Review market sizing logic, source quality, and capture assumptions for credibility and consistency.

prompt_templates.title: Define My Market
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for scope and inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define the market for [product]. My target customers are [customers], geography is [region], and business model is [model].

prompt_templates.title: Calculate TAM, SAM, and SOM
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TAM, SAM, and SOM estimate for [business idea]. Use bottom-up analysis first, then suggest a top-down validation method.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Assumptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this market sizing estimate for weak assumptions, missing filters, aggressive SOM, and source quality issues: [paste estimate].

prompt_templates.title: Build Investor Narrative
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this market sizing work into an investor-ready narrative with methodology, assumptions, triangulation, risks, and slide-ready takeaways: [paste analysis].

output_examples.input: Estimate the market for an AI email tool for North American e-commerce companies.
output: A bottom-up TAM built from target company counts and annual contract value.
A SAM narrowed by geography, AI readiness, and switching likelihood.
A year three and year five SOM based on realistic capture rates.

output_examples.input: Check whether my market sizing is credible for investors.
output: A review of market definition, source recency, and assumption consistency.
Flags for overbroad TAM, unsupported filters, and aggressive market share.
Suggested validation checks using public companies and alternate methods.

output_examples.input: Create a source plan for a B2B SaaS market estimate.
output: A prioritized list of public, paid, and primary research sources.
Guidance on citation details, source age, and methodology notes.
A reminder to triangulate customer counts, pricing, and penetration assumptions.

best_practices: Start with a precise customer, geography, product, and time horizon definition.
Use bottom-up calculations as the primary estimate when customer counts are available.
Document every assumption, source, filter, and adjustment in plain language.
anti_patterns: Do not present a broad industry TAM as the revenue opportunity you can actually serve.
Do not use stale or inconsistent data sources without explaining limitations.
Do not claim high SOM without evidence for distribution, differentiation, and execution capacity.

faq.question: What does this skill help calculate?
faq.answer: It helps calculate TAM, SAM, and SOM for business opportunities using structured market sizing methods.

faq.question: Which market sizing method should I start with?
faq.answer: Start with bottom-up analysis when customer counts and pricing are available. Use top-down analysis to validate the result.

faq.question: Can it create investor-ready market slides?
faq.answer: It can structure slide-ready market sizing content, but you should verify every number and source before presenting.

faq.question: Does it provide current market data?
faq.answer: No. It lists source types and research options, but users must collect and verify current data.

faq.question: Is this only for SaaS companies?
faq.answer: No. It includes guidance for SaaS, marketplaces, consumer markets, and B2B services.

faq.question: How should I handle uncertain assumptions?
faq.answer: Show ranges, document the basis for each assumption, and test sensitivity around price, customer count, and capture rate.
